title: AI Decision Trees — What to Do When You're Not Sure
subtitle: Step-by-step guidance for the situations that come up most often in Eduversal classrooms and offices

_meta.purpose: When you face a real situation and you're not sure what to do, open this page. Each decision tree walks you through the question step by step. Plain English. No jargon. Short sentences.
_meta.writingNote: These are not rules. They are guides. If the situation is unusual, talk to your line manager. The tree gets you most of the way; judgement gets you the rest.
_meta.toolRecommendationsRef: Tool-specific decisions reference manifest.toolRecommendations (tool list lives there as single source of truth).

howToReadADecisionTree.format: Each tree starts with a SITUATION (what's happening), then asks YES/NO QUESTIONS in order. Follow the path. The end of the path tells you the action to take.
howToReadADecisionTree.ifYouGetStuck: Some situations don't fit neatly. If the questions don't match your situation, look at the closest tree and use judgement. Or ask your Subject Leader / Academic Coordinator.
thingsTheseTreesAreNotFor: Safeguarding emergencies — those go through your school's safeguarding lead, not a flowchart.
Things explicitly forbidden in redFlagsAndRedlines.json — those are 'never', not 'it depends'.
Cambridge coursework integrity cases — those follow Cambridge's own procedures via the Exams Officers Guide.

shouldIUseAi.title: Should I use AI for this task?
shouldIUseAi.whoThisIsFor: Everyone, every time you're about to open an AI tool for school work
shouldIUseAi.situation: You have a task to do. You're wondering whether AI is a good fit.

steps.question: Will the result go directly into a report card, exam grade, or Cambridge submission without my own marking?
steps.yes: Stop. AI does not grade. Mark it yourself. (Red line 2.)
steps.no: Go to step 2.

steps.question: Am I about to type a real student's name, school ID, photo, or specific personal data?
steps.yes: Stop. Anonymise first ('a Year 8 student', 'a class with 2 EAL students'). Then come back. (Red line 1.)
steps.no: Go to step 3.

steps.question: Is this a decision about what should happen to a real student (set placement, intervention, discipline, referral)?
steps.yes: Stop. Real decisions about real students go through real people. Use the school's pastoral / SEN / academic structure. (Red line 5.)
steps.no: Go to step 4.

steps.question: Am I using a personal account, free trial, or non-school-approved AI tool?
steps.yes: Stop. Use only the school's approved tool for school work that touches any student data. (Red line 6.) See toolRecommendations in manifest.
steps.no: Go to step 5.

steps.question: Is this pattern-shaped drafting (lesson skeleton, differentiation, rubric draft, feedback template, summary, parent message draft)?
steps.yes: Yes — AI is a good fit. Use the relevant prompt from prompt-library.json. Review the output before using.
steps.no: Probably not a good AI task. Ask yourself: what part of this can AI help with that doesn't trigger one of the red lines above?

shouldIUseAi.endNotes: If you got to step 5 and the answer was 'yes', you're safe to proceed. Remember: AI drafts, you author. Always edit the output before using.

whichToolShouldIUse.title: Which AI tool should I use for this task?
whichToolShouldIUse.whoThisIsFor: Everyone — especially when starting out, when adding a new task type, or when one tool isn't giving good results
whichToolShouldIUse.situation: You've decided AI is a good fit for the task. Now: which tool?
whichToolShouldIUse.writingNote: Tool list lives in manifest.toolRecommendations as single source of truth. The three core options most likely approved network-wide in 2026: Claude for Education, ChatGPT for Teachers, Gemini for Education.

steps.question: Has my school officially approved an AI tool for staff use?
steps.yes: Use that tool by default. Even if it isn't the best for this specific task, the data privacy contract makes it the safer choice.
steps.no: Go to step 2. The most likely network-approved options for 2026 are: Claude for Education (Anthropic), ChatGPT for Teachers (OpenAI), or Gemini for Education (Google). Wait for confirmation before using any tool for student-context work.

steps.question: Will I touch any student-identifying data in this task, even anonymised?
steps.yes: Wait. Don't proceed until the school confirms an approved tool. Use the time to read the relevant section of your role playbook.
steps.no: Go to step 3. For non-student-context work (your own training, generic resource brainstorming, your own document summarisation), you have more flexibility, but still prefer school-tier products.

steps.question: Is this task about Bahasa Indonesia content (parent communication in Bahasa, Bahasa-language teaching materials, Indonesian cultural content)?
steps.yes: Per the 2025-2026 Bahasa Indonesia LLM benchmark, Gemini 3.x and Claude Opus 4.6 are the strongest. ChatGPT is correct but more formal than typical Indonesian parent register. Pick based on what your school has approved.
steps.no: Go to step 4.

steps.question: Is this task about Cambridge syllabus alignment, AO mapping, or coursework-related work?
steps.yes: Any of the core-three educator-tier tools work. For multi-document Cambridge bulletin analysis, NotebookLM is the standout — load multiple bulletins and syllabus versions, ask cross-document questions. Always verify Cambridge AO citations against the actual syllabus — every tool gets these wrong sometimes.
steps.no: Go to step 5.

steps.question: What kind of task is it specifically?

branches.differentiation_or_worksheets: Best dedicated tool: MagicSchool AI (school tier). Faster than general chat for routine differentiation. Diffit if you work from textbook passages. Brisk Teaching if you're inside Google Docs.
branches.rubric_or_feedback_drafting: MagicSchool's Rubric Generator and Text Feedback are bundled with everything else. Eduaide.ai if you want a simpler privacy footprint (teacher-only login).
branches.student_facing_classroom_activity: Never a general chatbot. Use SchoolAI (teacher dashboard, all student chats visible) for most ages. Khanmigo for maths specifically. Curipod for younger years.
branches.parent_communication: Claude for Education for the draft. DeepL Pro for the Bahasa Indonesia verification pass.
branches.slides_or_visuals: Canva for Education + Magic Studio (safest school tier). Adobe Express for Education as an alternative.
branches.meeting_notes_or_document_analysis: NotebookLM for multi-document. Claude or ChatGPT for general summarisation. Otter.ai for live meeting transcription (Pro tier minimum, NOT for confidential meetings).
branches.item_drafting_for_practice_bank_only: Wayground (formerly Quizizz AI) for bulk MCQ. Questgen for Bloom-tagged items. NEVER for formal pipeline.
branches.translation: DeepL Pro for EN ↔ Bahasa parent letters. Google Translate only for quick short checks.

steps.default: Use whatever your school has approved from the core three. The differences between Claude / ChatGPT / Gemini for general work are smaller than the difference between using AI well and using AI badly.

whichToolShouldIUse.endNotes: Most educators settle on one main tool within the first month. That's normal. Switching tools every week stops you from getting good at any of them. Find one that fits your workflow and stay with it. Add specialised tools (MagicSchool, NotebookLM, etc.) only when a specific workflow demands them.

studentSubmissionLooksAi.title: A student's work looks like AI wrote it. What do I do?
studentSubmissionLooksAi.whoThisIsFor: Subject teachers, subject leaders, Cambridge coordinators
studentSubmissionLooksAi.situation: A student has submitted work that doesn't sound like them, or that an AI detector flagged, or that someone reported as suspicious.

steps.question: Is this Cambridge coursework (IGCSE, AS/A Level, or other Cambridge-graded work)?
steps.yes: Stop. Cambridge has its own procedure. Refer to the Cambridge Exams Officers Guide Phase 3 and contact your Cambridge Coordinator BEFORE talking to the student. Do not handle this as a normal academic integrity case.
steps.no: Go to step 2.

steps.question: Did an AI detector tool flag this work?
steps.yes: STOP. An AI detector result is NOT proof. For Eduversal's EAL student population, detector tools are especially unreliable: a 2023 Patterns study found 7 detectors flagged 61% of non-native English essays as AI-generated. Turnitin AI detection has ~30% higher false-positive rate for ESL writers. Major universities (Vanderbilt, Johns Hopkins, Northwestern, UCLA, UC San Diego, Yale, Waterloo, Oregon State) disabled Turnitin AI detection in late 2025 for this reason. Treat the flag as zero signal — do not even mention it in the conversation with the student. Go to step 3.
steps.no: Go to step 3.

steps.question: Have I talked to the student yet?
steps.yes: Go to step 5.
steps.no: Go to step 4. Do NOT accuse before talking.

steps.question: What do I say in the conversation?
guidance: Start neutral: 'I want to ask you about this piece of work. Can you walk me through how you wrote it?'
Ask process questions: 'What did you start with? Why did you choose this argument? Where did you get this example from?'
Don't accuse. Let the student speak.
A student who wrote the work can usually explain their choices. A student who didn't usually can't — or contradicts themselves.
If the student volunteers that they used AI, ask how. Some uses are fine (asking AI to help understand a concept). Some aren't (asking AI to write the whole thing).

steps.question: After the conversation, am I confident the work was AI-generated?
steps.yes: Follow your school's academic integrity policy. Document the conversation, the evidence, and the outcome. Do NOT improvise consequences.
steps.no: Accept the work. Adjust how you assess similar tasks in future — more in-class drafting, more visible process, asking students to share drafts mid-task. Make AI shortcuts less attractive by design.

studentSubmissionLooksAi.endNotes: False accusations damage trust badly. A student wrongly accused of AI use often disengages for months. The cost of a wrong accusation is much higher than the cost of one missed AI submission.

parentAsksWhyWeUseAi.title: A parent asks why the school uses AI. What do I say?
parentAsksWhyWeUseAi.whoThisIsFor: Subject teachers, subject leaders, school leaders — anyone who talks to parents
parentAsksWhyWeUseAi.situation: A parent — anxious, curious, or already informed — asks you why or how the school uses AI.

steps.question: What kind of parent question is this?

options.kind: Anxious (worried about cheating, screen time, child safety)
Curious (wants to know what's happening in their child's school)
Informed (already uses AI at home, expects the school to match)

whatToSay: Acknowledge their concern first. 'I understand — many parents are thinking about this.'
Be specific about what the school does NOT do: 'We don't use AI to grade your child's work. Teachers do that.'
Be specific about what the school does: 'Teachers use AI to help plan lessons faster, so they have more time for your child. Students learn to use AI critically — to question it, not just trust it.'
Cite authorities: 'We follow Cambridge International's guidance and Indonesia's data protection law.'
Offer to follow up: 'If you have more questions, we have a session on this topic in [Term X], or I can put you in touch with our Academic Coordinator.'
doNotDoThis: Don't promise the school is doing more than it actually is.
Don't argue about whether AI is good or bad — meet the parent where they are.

whatToSay: Share concrete examples: 'In maths, I use AI to make practice problems at different levels — that way every student gets work that fits them.'
Be honest about limits: 'We don't let AI grade work. We don't use it to make decisions about your child. Those things stay with the teachers.'

Mention the bigger picture: 'Indonesia's Permendikdasmen 13/2025 makes Koding and AI an elective. Cambridge International refreshed its digital literacy curriculum in 2026. Your child is being prepared for a world where AI is normal — we'd rather teach them to use it well than pretend it doesn't exist.'
doNotDoThis: Don't use technical jargon (LLM, neural network, etc.).

whatToSay: Be specific about which tools the school uses (or is in the process of approving).
Acknowledge what you don't know yet: 'We are still working out our approach for [specific issue]. Cambridge is publishing new guidance and we're aligning to it.'
Welcome their input: 'If your child is using AI tools at home that we don't know about, I'd be interested to hear what's working.'
doNotDoThis: Don't pretend to know more than you do.
Don't get defensive if they ask hard questions.

parentAsksWhyWeUseAi.endNotes: Parents talk to each other. The same message goes to all three kinds of parent eventually. Be consistent. Honest uncertainty beats false confidence every time.

staffMemberMisusedAi.title: A staff member broke an AI rule. What do I do?
staffMemberMisusedAi.whoThisIsFor: School principals, academic coordinators, subject leaders, foundation representatives, directors
staffMemberMisusedAi.situation: You've found out (or someone has reported) that a staff member used AI in a way that breaks Eduversal AI policy or school AI policy.

steps.question: What kind of breach is it?

options.kind: Low harm (e.g. used a personal ChatGPT account to draft a lesson plan, no student data involved)
Moderate harm (e.g. typed a student's name into a public AI tool, sent unedited AI-drafted parent message)
Serious harm (e.g. AI-graded summative assessments, AI-authored chapter test items submitted as formal, repeated breaches after warning)

action: Have a coaching conversation. Not a disciplinary one.
Reference the relevant red line in redFlagsAndRedlines.json. Ask the staff member to read it.
Make sure the staff member knows which tool they SHOULD use (point to manifest.toolRecommendations).
Document the conversation in your own notes. No formal record yet.
Follow up in two weeks — confirm the new practice is in place.
Identify what student data was exposed (if any) and assess whether the school's data protection process needs to be triggered (under UU PDP).
Mandatory retraining: the staff member completes the relevant playbook section and self-attests.
Track for repeat. If it happens again, treat as serious harm.
If the breach is serious enough to trigger UU PDP processes, your school's data protection officer handles that — escalate.
Follow your school's normal HR / performance management process. The Eduversal AI Framework v1.0 is the cited authority.
If student data was exposed and the school's UU PDP processes apply, the data protection officer is the lead — your role is to provide evidence.
If Cambridge coursework integrity was compromised, the Cambridge Coordinator triggers Cambridge's own procedures.
If the staff member is in a leadership role, the Foundation Representative or Eduversal Academic Directorate may need to be informed depending on severity.

staffMemberMisusedAi.endNotes: The point is not to punish — it's to protect students and the school. Most AI misuse is from staff who didn't know better. Make sure they know better next time. Repeated misuse after clear guidance is a different matter and is handled through normal performance management.

shouldOurSchoolAdoptThisTool.title: Should our school adopt this AI tool?
shouldOurSchoolAdoptThisTool.whoThisIsFor: School principals, foundation representatives, academic coordinators
shouldOurSchoolAdoptThisTool.situation: A vendor has approached the school, a teacher is asking for it, or you're reviewing tools for the new academic year.

steps.question: Does the vendor offer a school-tier (education-tier) contract that excludes our data from model training?
steps.yes: Go to step 2.
steps.no: Stop. Do not adopt. Free-tier or personal-tier products are not safe for school-wide deployment with student data.

steps.question: Where does the tool process data — inside Indonesia, or cross-border with documented adequacy safeguards under UU PDP?
steps.yes: Go to step 3.
steps.no: Caution. Most major AI tools process data outside Indonesia. UU PDP allows this but the burden of proof is on you. Document the safeguards before proceeding.

steps.question: Does this tool solve a problem we actually have, or is it a 'solution looking for a problem'?
guidance: Be honest. Sometimes new tools are exciting but don't solve a current need.
If the answer is 'yes, but it would be nice to have', defer until next year.
If the answer is 'yes, this addresses [specific staff or student need]', go to step 4.

steps.question: Do we have the technical capacity to deploy this — onboarding, training, ongoing support?
steps.yes: Go to step 5.
steps.no: Pilot with one department first. Don't roll out school-wide until support capacity is in place.

steps.question: Is the cost justified by the expected benefit?
guidance: Look at the full annual cost, not the introductory price.
Compare against the cost of doing without the tool.
Be aware of the 'paying for a logo' trap — paying premium for brand recognition rather than features.
steps.decision: If yes to all five steps, proceed with adoption (with documented contract, staff training plan, and rollout schedule). Add the tool to the approved-tools register. Update manifest.toolRecommendations for the network if it's a strong choice.

shouldOurSchoolAdoptThisTool.endNotes: Don't adopt more than one or two new tools per academic year. Teachers need time to get good at tools. Constant tool changes mean nobody gets fluent at anything.

promptIsntWorking.title: My AI prompt isn't giving me useful output. What do I do?
promptIsntWorking.whoThisIsFor: Everyone — especially teachers in their first months of regular AI use
promptIsntWorking.situation: You used a prompt. The output isn't what you wanted. You're not sure if the prompt is wrong, the tool is wrong, or you're asking for the wrong thing.

steps.question: Did you give the AI enough context — your role, year group, syllabus, class profile, constraints?
steps.yes: Go to step 2.
steps.no: Add the missing context and try again. The 4-part mega-prompt (Role + Context + Task + Constraints) consistently produces better output than one-line questions.

steps.question: Did you only ask once?
steps.yes: Follow up. Iteration is where good output comes from. Ask: 'Where in this output is weak?' / 'Can you give me three alternative versions?' / 'What's missing from this?'
steps.no: Go to step 3.

steps.question: Are you asking AI for something it's bad at (specific Indonesian factual details, Cambridge AO citations, judgement about a real student)?
steps.yes: Stop. AI is bad at these. Use a different approach — check the source yourself, ask a colleague, use professional judgement.
steps.no: Go to step 4.

steps.question: Is the prompt from prompt-library.json or one you wrote yourself?
steps.yes: Check the prompt-library notes for that prompt — 'pitfalls' section often explains what goes wrong.
steps.no: Try the matching prompt from prompt-library.json. The library prompts are tested. Custom prompts often miss something the tested ones include.

steps.question: Have you tried a different tool?
guidance: Sometimes one tool is just better at a specific task type. Claude tends to be more cautious and analytical. ChatGPT tends to be more enthusiastic and creative. Gemini tends to be more structured.
If your school has approved more than one tool, try the same prompt in a different one and compare.

promptIsntWorking.endNotes: Most 'AI didn't work' situations are actually 'my prompt was too short' or 'I gave up after the first answer'. The Practitioner-level skill is iteration. Try the same prompt three times with refinements before you decide AI can't do this.

myFreeTrialIsEnding.title: My personal AI subscription is ending — should I keep paying for school work?
myFreeTrialIsEnding.whoThisIsFor: Subject teachers, subject leaders — the people most likely to be self-funding their AI use
myFreeTrialIsEnding.situation: You have a personal Claude / ChatGPT / Gemini subscription. The trial period is ending or the cost is going up. You're wondering whether to keep paying out of pocket for school work.

steps.question: Has your school approved a tool for staff use yet?
steps.yes: Use that one. Stop paying personally. The school's tool has a data protection contract — your personal subscription doesn't.
steps.no: Go to step 2.

steps.question: Has the school told you when an approved tool will be available?
steps.yes: Use only AI for non-student-context work in the meantime (your own training, generic resource brainstorming). Don't put student data through personal accounts. Wait for the school tool.
steps.no: Raise it to your Academic Coordinator. The school owes its staff a clear answer on this. Until then, restrict AI use to non-student work.

myFreeTrialIsEnding.endNotes: It's tempting to keep paying personally because the tool is useful. But the data protection issue is real under UU PDP. Personal-account use of public AI for student work creates a compliance issue for the school whether the school knows or not. Wait for the approved tool.

amIReadyForPractitionerLevel.title: Am I ready to move from Foundation to Practitioner level?
amIReadyForPractitionerLevel.whoThisIsFor: Subject teachers preparing for their annual self-assessment
amIReadyForPractitionerLevel.situation: You've been using AI in your teaching. You're wondering if you've moved past Foundation to Practitioner.

steps.question: Do you use AI confidently for at least 3-4 different workflows (e.g. lesson planning + differentiation + feedback + parent communication)?
steps.yes: Go to step 2.
steps.no: Still Foundation. Pick one more workflow to develop this term.

steps.question: Do you iterate with AI — follow up, refine, ask for alternatives — rather than accepting the first answer?
steps.yes: Go to step 3.
steps.no: Still Foundation. This is the biggest single difference between Foundation and Practitioner — iteration habit.

steps.question: Have you taught at least one student-facing AI literacy lesson (e.g. critique an AI output) in your subject?
steps.yes: Go to step 4.
steps.no: Close to Practitioner but not yet. Run one student-facing AI literacy activity this term.

steps.question: Can you spot subtle AI errors — cultural bias, fabricated facts, weak distractors — before your students or colleagues do?
steps.yes: Go to step 5.
steps.no: Close to Practitioner. Spend more time noticing the AI errors you currently let pass.

steps.question: Do you share what you've learned with colleagues — informally or formally?
steps.yes: You're at Practitioner. Mark Practitioner on your self-assessment.
steps.no: Almost. Practitioner involves contributing back, not just personal use. Share two prompts with a colleague this term and you're there.

amIReadyForPractitionerLevel.endNotes: Honest self-assessment matters more than ambition. Foundation-with-strong-growth is more valuable than inflated-Practitioner. Your line manager validates your self-assessment — inflated ratings will be adjusted down, and that's a worse outcome than honest Foundation.

treesWeNeedButDontHaveYet._purpose: Decision trees we've identified as needed but haven't written yet. Listed so future editing rounds know what to add.
items: How to introduce AI in my classroom for the first time — for teachers who have never used AI with students before.
What to do when AI gives me an answer that contradicts the textbook — for teachers who are confused about which source to trust.
What to do if Cambridge updates its AI guidance mid-year — for Cambridge Coordinators.
What to do when a parent asks for their child to be excluded from AI use — for school principals.
What to do when student AI use in coursework is widespread in my class — for subject teachers and Cambridge Coordinators.
What to do if your school does NOT have a Cambridge Coordinator (smaller schools) — for school principals navigating Cambridge guidance alone.

v1.0_2026-05-16.added: Initial release with 9 decision trees covering the most common ambiguous situations across all roles.
v1.0_2026-05-16.knownGaps: See treesWeNeedButDontHaveYet — 6 additional trees planned for v1.1 (Term 2 update).
v1.0_2026-05-16.pendingPopulation: whichToolShouldIUse tree currently references manifest.toolRecommendations as a placeholder. Specific tool names will be filled in once the tool list is populated (in progress — background research task).
